stem: Let \( f(x) = x^{3} + ([[A]])x^{2} + ([[B]])x + ([[C]]) \). Which of the following is a real root of \( f \)?
solution: By construction, \( f(x) = (x-([[r]]))(x-([[p]]))(x-([[q]])) \), so its real roots are \( [[r]], [[p]], [[q]] \); one valid root is \( x = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This reads the \( x^{2} \)-coefficient directly rather than solving the cubic for an actual root.
This negates the \( x^{2} \)-coefficient, which is the SUM of the roots, not a root itself.

This negates the constant term, which is the PRODUCT of the roots, not a root itself.

stem: Let \( p(x) = x^{2} + ([[B]])x + ([[C]]) \). What is the sum of the roots of \( p \)?
solution: By Vieta's formulas, for \( p(x) = x^{2} + ([[B]])x + ([[C]]) \), the sum of the roots is \( -([[B]]) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This reads the linear coefficient directly and forgets that the sum of roots is its negative.
This reports the constant term instead of the (negated) linear coefficient.
This negates the constant term, which is the product of the roots, not their sum.
This adds the two coefficients together, which has no basis in Vieta's formulas.
This is off by one from the correct sum.

stem: Let \( p(x) = x^{3} + ([[A]])x^{2} + ([[B]])x + ([[C]]) \), which has three real roots. What is the product of the roots of \( p \)?
solution: By Vieta's formulas, for a monic cubic \( x^{3} + ([[A]])x^{2} + ([[B]])x + ([[C]]) \), the product of the roots equals \( -([[C]]) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This reads the constant term directly and forgets that the product of roots is its negative.
This gives the (negated) sum of the roots instead of their product.
This gives the sum of pairwise products of the roots, not the product of all three.
This negates the sum of pairwise products, which is unrelated to the product of all three roots.
This is off by one from the correct product.

stem: Let \( p(x) = [[a]]x^{2} + ([[b]])x + ([[c]]) \) and \( q(x) = [[a]]x^{2} + ([[m]])x + ([[k]]) \). At what \( x \)-value do the graphs of \( p \) and \( q \) intersect?
solution: Setting \( p(x) = q(x) \), the \( x^{2} \) terms cancel (both have leading coefficient [[a]]), leaving the linear equation \( ([[b]])x + ([[c]]) = ([[m]])x + ([[k]]) \), so \( x = \dfrac{[[k]]-([[c]])}{[[b]]-([[m]])} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This reports the difference of the constant terms alone, forgetting to divide by the difference of the linear coefficients.
This has the numerator's sign flipped relative to the correct ratio.
This has the ratio inverted (numerator and denominator swapped in sign convention).
This reports only the difference of the linear coefficients, not the full ratio.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

solution: First evaluate the inner function: \( q([[x0]]) = [[m]]([[x0]]) + ([[n]]) \). Then apply \( p \) to that result: \( p(q([[x0]])) = [[a]]\big(q([[x0]])\big)^{2} + ([[b]]) = [[answer]] \).

This forgets to evaluate the inner function \( q \) at all, applying \( p \) directly to \( [[x0]] \).
This reports \( q([[x0]]) \) itself, forgetting to also apply the outer function \( p \).
This forgets to add the shift \( [[n]] \) inside the inner function before squaring.

stem: Let \( f(x) = \dfrac{(x - ([[p]]))(x - ([[q]]))}{(x - ([[p]]))(x - ([[r]]))} \), which has a removable hole at \( x = [[p]] \). What is \( f([[x0]]) \)?
solution: For \( x \ne [[p]] \), the common factor \( x-([[p]]) \) cancels, so \( f(x) = \dfrac{x-([[q]])}{x-([[r]])} \). Substituting \( x=[[x0]] \) (which is not the hole) gives \( f([[x0]]) = \dfrac{[[x0]]-([[q]])}{[[x0]]-([[r]])} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the ORIGINAL uncancelled form; it reduces to the same value algebraically but the computation should use the simplified function.
This uses the cancelled factor's root \( [[p]] \) in the numerator instead of the surviving root \( [[q]] \).
This inverts which root belongs to the numerator versus the denominator after cancellation.
This inverts the entire simplified fraction.

stem: Let \( r(x) = \dfrac{[[a]]x + ([[b]])}{[[c]]x + ([[d]])} \). What value does \( r(x) \) approach as \( x \to \infty \)?
solution: As \( x\to\infty \), the numerator and denominator are dominated by their linear terms, so \( r(x) \to \dfrac{[[a]]}{[[c]]} = [[answer]] \), the ratio of the leading coefficients.
